data.title: Sell MSOS And Its 3 Largest Holdings
data.description:
I was optimistic for the multi-state operators when cannabis rescheduling by the DEA was underway, and I upgraded to Neutral some of them and the world's largest cannabis ETF, the AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (NYSEARCA:MSOS). Things have not appeared to be moving towards the elimination of 280E taxation, which will be devastating to the American cannabis industry if it remains. Today, I am downgrading three large MSOs, and I am also downgrading MSOS again.

When I wrote about MSOS four weeks ago, I did downgrade it to sell and say that it was at big risk. While it is down since then, I am changing my rating to Strong Sell. I am also downgrading its three largest holdings, Curaleaf (OTCPK:CURLF), Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF) and Trulieve (OTCQX:TCNNF).

MSOS Is Still In a Bear Market

MSOS has dropped a ton, but it is not at the low. In fact, this move last week helped it to fill an open gap that had been left in early April:

MSOS and its MSO holdings all plunged in November when Florida failed to pass adult-use legalization, which needed 60% of voters but fell short, and Trump was elected President. I had thought the market was overdone then, but it has continued to sell off.

Over the past 4+ years, MSOS has plunged almost 90%, and it has pulled back even more from its peak:

Trading volumes have plunged too. Few seem to care about this ETF compared to how many did in the past.

MSOS Has Seen Redemptions

At the beginning of the year, MSOS had 130.575 million shares outstanding. Today, there are 132.125 million, so the share count has increased by 1.2%. This is after it rose 52.5% in 2024. Shares have decreased since March 7th by 3.4%. This is not that bad considering how much the price has declined since then (11.7%) and year-to-date (32.8%). Since August 2023, shares have increased, but the one-year growth of 14.1% is the lowest it has been and down from the peak of 94% during the summer of 2024:

So, the one-year growth in shares outstanding has plunged, and the number of shares outstanding has dropped since early March.

Sell MSOS and Its Top Holdings

MSOS does not trade at a big premium to its net asset value, but it has rallied sharply from its all-time low earlier this month. The current price of $2.56 is a penny above its NAV and is up 26.7% from its all-time intraday low of $2.02. The bounces at its three top holdings have been 45.2% for Curaleaf, 21.0% for GTI and 28.2% for Trulieve. Of course, MSOS is down a lot in 2025, falling 32.8%. CURLF has dropped 37.2%, GTBIF has fallen 31.5%, and TCNNF is down 25.3%. The overall cannabis sector has fallen year-to-date by 28.1% as measured by the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index.

Year-to-date, MSOS has seen its shares outstanding increase by 1.2%, though they fell last week again and in March too. The redemptions this past week hit each of the three top holdings. Year-to-date, MSOS exposure to GTBIF has dropped by 11.8%. It has increased its exposure to CURLF shares by 7.0%, and it has decreased exposure to TCNNF shares by 1.3%. Here is the current portfolio's top-10 holdings:

These top three stocks currently comprise 68.7% of the ETF, which is extremely high. There are three other names that are above 5% each, and the top seven names represent 93.1% of the ETF, which is fully invested (actually, slightly over-invested).

If MSOS sees more redemptions, it will be forced to sell these three largest names, all of which dropped this week. As a reminder, in late 2022 and into 2023, MSOS saw very large redemptions. From 12/16/22 to 03/31/23, shares outstanding dropped 13.2%. At that time, MSOS owned some non-MSOs, and it used this period to eliminate those holdings.

Looking at these three large holdings, they all trade at seemingly low valuations based on the consensus at Koyfin. Curaleaf currently has an enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA in 2025 of just 4.0X. GTI trades at 4.1X, and Trulieve trades at just 2.3X. From this table below of the Top 5 MSOs, which include also Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF) and Verano (OTCQX:VRNOF), they also have low valuations for the trailing year as well as 2026:

While GTI does have positive tangible book value, the rest are negative. They all have net debt, especially Curaleaf. These companies are at risk of 280E remaining in place. Of course, EBITDA does not take their very high levels of taxation. Each of these companies is projected to have negative EPS in 2025 (CURLF: -$0.25 and TCNNF: -$0.20). GTBIF currently has a projection of $0.28 in 2025 and then $0.31 for 2026. The stock, at $5.60, has a 2026 PE of 18X.

While I upgraded GTBIF at a much higher price almost a year ago, I do not think it should be held and I am downgrading it to sell. The stock is still above its 2020 low, which is unlike any of its peers. I am not sure if it will get below $4.00, but my target for year-end is much lower at $3.72 assuming a PE of 12X for 2026. I respect the balance sheet, but the growth has been quite slow. The state-regulated cannabis system is very complex, and M&A can be quite challenging. So, the weakness across the industry won't lead to GTBIF buying out weak competitors generally unless the rules change.

I upgraded CURLF to Neutral in December, and I am downgrading it to Strong Sell. The debt level is extremely high, and the tangible book value is extremely negative at -$853 million. My target in that last article for year-end was $4.45, but I am lowering it dramatically now. In March, ahead of their Q4 financial report, I shared a target with 420 Investor subscribers of $1.35 based on 4X enterprise value to projected adjusted EBITDA for 2026. That estimate fell sharply, and, using that same multiple, I now get $1.17, which is up 19%. This is not a lot of upside, and there is tremendous risk for this company.

I never upgraded TCNNF to Neutral, but I am lowering my rating to Strong Sell. In March, I reiterated it as a Sell when it was $4.24. In that March article, I shared a target for year-end of $7.34 based on a 4X enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA ratio. I did point out that my target may have been too aggressive due to its tax liabilities, and I am lowering that now to 3X. This works out to $4.97, which is 28% higher. I am extremely negative about Florida, which is where it does a lot of its business.

High debt levels and uncertain tax liabilities stand out for Curaleaf and Trulieve, and all three of these MSOs have big exposure to Florida. I think that their entry into beverages with THC derived from hemp could be a good thing, and am glad to see them expand beyond the dispensary-only model, but I don't think that the beverages will move the needle. Plus, the state governments are moving to shut down these types of beverages. The biggest risk to my negativity is that the federal government takes action to eliminate 280E taxation.

Yes, these stocks are all down a lot since April 30th, when the DEA announced that it was working on rescheduling:

Going back to late August 2023, their declines have been less, though all are down except for Trulieve:

Trulieve was very cheap then, which was right ahead of the rescheduling recommendation by the Department of Health & Human Services. Sure, buying dips can work, but I don't think this is yet the time to own MSOS or these three MSOs.

Conclusion

I have not gone to a Buy rating on MSOS or its three top holdings despite the big plunge in prices, but even at these lower prices I don't find any reason to own any of these. Yes, they are cheap, but this is not a reason to buy unless one thinks that things will change.

The bounce this past week, with MSOS up four straight days, is related probably to 4/20, which will be celebrated on Easter Sunday, or perhaps the introduction in the House of Representatives of STATES 2.0 Act, which was announced on 4/17 and has some good ideas. So, STATES 2.0 Act, which is just proposed in the House, might be good, but, for now, it is too early to just assume it will pass.

Again, for those who want to own cannabis stocks, I have shared many ideas here. Currently, I still believe that for those who want to own (or need to own) an ETF, CNBS is a much better option than MSOS. While I have a Buy rating on the Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), I am not so sure that it will go up a lot. It is down since that piece, but by a bit less than MSOS.

For those who want to own MSOs, I have some better ideas than CURLF, GTBIF and TCNNF. I also believe that there are opportunities outside of the MSO sector, such as some Canadian LPs and Ancillary cannabis companies. I believe in cannabis as medicine and a good alternative to alcohol, but I don't believe in all cannabis stocks all of the time. Right now, I am quite fearful for MSOS and its three largest holdings.

data.title: 5 Dividend Growth Stocks I'm Buying As Defensive Sectors Become Sexy Again
data.description:
The stock market panic attack appears to be over, at least for now.

Investors have shifted from a mindset of "sell everything that isn't nailed to the floor" to a distinctly more selective approach. Most of what euphorically rallied in 2023-2024 is now unwinding, and defensive stalwarts have stolen the momentum.

While my surmising about a potential redux of the Dot Com bubble burst last year seemed far-fetched (to some) at the time, it now seems like an increasingly apt comparison.

In what follows, I'll split the article into two sections:

  • Big picture thoughts on market rotation, the crashing dollar, US vs. non-US stocks, and an unfortunate consequence of tariffs and trade wars.
  • Some thoughts on my shrinking buy list, especially a few particularly notable names on the list.

Onward!

Big Picture Thoughts

Wow. Defensive stocks are actually kind of sexy again.

Just a few months ago, it would've been unfathomable that the roles of boring defensive sectors and AI-related tech stocks would completely reverse soon.

As tariffs have increasingly been threatened and implemented over the last few months, there has been a stark divergence in the market. This divergence has massively benefited my defensive, real estate-heavy portfolio.

Year to date, the real estate stocks in the S&P 500 (XLRE) and to a lesser extent the real estate sector more broadly (VNQ) have significantly outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY), the Nasdaq index (QQQ), and especially the Magnificent 7 (MAGS):

It's shocking to see the Mag 7, which have massively led the market in returns over the last few years, down over 20% YTD.

On the other hand, much of those returns over the last few years were fueled by multiple expansions, rendering them richly valued relative to the rest of the market. Higher valuations, as we've rediscovered this year, make stocks more vulnerable to panic-selling during periods of uncertainty.

While hard data on the effect of the tariffs is limited, so far, this uncertainty-driven selloff has catalyzed a major rotation from the tech (XLK) and consumer discretionary (XLY) sectors into defensive sectors like real estate, consumer staples (XLP), utilities (XLU), and healthcare (XLV).

Note that the healthcare sector would be doing better than real estate YTD if not for UnitedHealth Group's (UNH) ~23% plunge on Thursday, April 17th, which was triggered by the health insurer's Q1 earnings miss and guidance cut.

Not coincidentally, the parts of the market doing most poorly this year are generally those with the most foreign sales exposure.

Under normal circumstances, you would think this is odd, considering the fact that the US dollar is crashing and theoretically making American-made products more attractive.

But actually, the dollar is crashing for basically the same reason, as are US companies with high foreign sales exposure. Both are expressions of the international backlash against the US for its recent tariff policies.

Both US tariffs and retaliatory tariffs from foreign governments have created a huge disruption that has surprisingly been better for non-US stocks (VEU) than it has for US stocks (VTI):

Note that tariffs aren't the only force at play here. There has been a tremendous amount of bad will triggered toward the US in recent months that is resulting in negative economic effects in the US.

Bloomberg reports that international boycotts of US products as well as a decline in foreign tourism in the US will likely cause about a $90 billion hit to US GDP. At the beginning of this year, the US was on pace for another record year for international arrivals at US airports. But in March, international air arrivals dropped 10% year-over-year.

The bad will associated with the trade war appears to be almost completely one-sided. The US is seen as the aggressor and therefore foreigners are boycotting US products, but there does not seem to be any corresponding boycott in the US of foreign-made products.

If anything, the opposite is the case in the US; Americans are stockpiling foreign-made products for fear of future price hikes.

I've discussed in past reports the boycott of Tesla (TSLA) cars, especially in Canada and parts of Europe. This mirrors a serious boycott movement in the United States, which has resulted in a huge surge in used Tesla cars in the market as of March:

With a refreshed Model Y just hitting the market and the threat of an end to EV tax credits to motivate buyers, you'd expect to see more demand for Teslas right now, not less.

Most boycotts have little discernible effect, which makes this one notable.

Likewise, the Wall Street Journal reports that in China, there has been a grassroots shift among consumers away from American products:

From smartphones to fast food, major American brands are rapidly losing market share in China to domestic rivals.

For example, Tesla sales in China were down 12% in Q1 2025, which allowed Tesla's Chinese EV rival BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) to extend its growing lead in global car sales:

And for the quintessentially American shoe brand, Nike (NKE), sales declined 17% YoY in China.

Hollywood could be a significant casualty of this trade war, as China is a major market for American-made movies. Although there is an official restriction on film imports from the US by the Chinese government, there appears (based on the WSJ article) to be a grassroots movement to boycott American films as well.

The Smoot-Hawley tariffs of 1930 incited a similar sort of economic nationalism across the world, as I wrote about in "5 Stocks & 2 ETFs I'm Buying As The Specter of Recession Looms."

It's interesting -- and sad -- to see history repeating like this.

My DGI Buy List Today

Given my investment strategy of buying and holding high-quality dividend growth stocks for the long term, my portfolio tends to have a heavily defensive tilt.

Thus, a consequence of defensive sectors making a resurgence this year is the fact that my buy list is narrowing.

If you imagine a Venn diagram, my buy list is basically the overlap between undervaluation and high conviction/confidence.

As I discussed in last week's article, some DGI stocks that are undervalued in my view come with some “known unknowns” and “unknown unknowns” that prevent me from gaining the conviction I'd want to buy them.

A prominent example is Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE), owner and developer of Class A life science campuses in every major research hub in the country. Normally, being an industry leader with a #1 or #2 market position and a strong balance sheet would be enough to weather any storm. But the oversupply in life science real estate right now is huge, and at the same time, demand for space may very well pull back this year amid the layoffs of FDA personnel.

A WSJ article reveals that after the layoffs of FDA employees involved in the drug approval process, there have been significant delays in the progress of drugs in the pipeline.

Significant delays in the FDA’s core functions—such as approving amendments to clinical trials and guiding companies through processes for drug approval—are hindering the ability to develop drugs, say industry officials. Those setbacks are contributing to drugs taking longer to get through clinical trials and ultimately reach patients—and straining dollars for testing new possible treatments, say people familiar with the matter.

A second-order effect of this slowdown in the drug approval process is likely to be less demand for US life science/lab space. Some of this R&D will move to European or Canadian labs, and some of it simply won't be done at all.

I don't know how significant this will be yet, which is why I can't gain the conviction to buy more ARE right now, despite it being bargain-basement priced at about 9x FFO.

While ARE's long-term outlook is cloudy, I feel much more confident about these five stocks on my buy list:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Blackstone (BX) ~3% Variable (Avg. High-Single-Digit)
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.5% High Single-Digit to Low Double-Digit
Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) 0.5% Double Digit
Hannon Armstrong Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI) 6.9% Mid-Single-Digit
NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE) 3.4% High-Single-Digit

Since most of these were on my buy list last week, I'll just comment on two of them.

Blackstone

BX just reported pretty solid Q1 2025 earnings, including 11% YoY growth in distributable earnings (DE) per share and $61.6 billion of AUM inflows, up by about $4 billion quarter-over-quarter.

Total AUM sat at $1.17 trillion at the end of Q1, and management is aiming for $1.2 trillion by the end of the year. That implies a slowing rate of net AUM growth over the rest of this year, which isn't terribly surprising given the uncertainty in the capital markets.

Performance in private equity and credit were both strong with Q1 DE growth of 13% and 76%, respectively, which offset continued weakness in the real estate segment (-20% DE YoY). It's nice to have a diversified portfolio of non-correlated assets to offset each other during wild macroeconomic swings.

As long as interest rates remain stable or declining going forward, I believe BX's real estate segment should reverse its recent streak of poor performance and become a winner.

At a price to TTM DE of 27.7x, but based on this year's DE, the company is likely valued at around 25-26x DE.

Is that cheap in an absolute sense? No. Is it a more reasonable valuation than the ~30x DE that BX was valued at for much of last year? Yes. Is it a fair price to pay for a capital-light asset manager with strong long-term growth prospects and a shareholder-friendly dividend policy? I think so.

Alphabet

I think the primary reason why GOOGL is down ~20% YTD and now trades at a remarkably reasonable 17x earnings is fear that AI chatbots will completely replace the company's biggest cash machine: Google Search.

Last year, Google handled an average of 14 billion searches per day, and Search is much easier to monetize than AI chatbots like Gemini. Plus, Google continues to maintain about 90% market share of Internet searches.

And yet, AI usership is exploding. Adoption of this new technology has been even faster than for computers or the Internet.

Anecdotally, there are lots of cases of people abandoning Search entirely in exchange for AI chatbots.

On the other hand, my own anecdotal experience suggests a hybrid model is more likely. For me, AI chatbots have been more of an additive than a tradeoff with Google Search. I view them as different tools with different but mostly complementary uses. If I want to know about the weather or what movies are playing in our local theater, I use Google Search. If I want a broad overview of a topic or a summary of a company's earnings report, I use an AI chatbot.

In short, I think that if AI eats into Google Search's market share at all, that should be more than offset by Alphabet's continued growth in other categories, such as YouTube, Google Cloud, and Waymo.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - April 2025
data.description:
Note: Please note that the stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

It may sound like an understatement to say that the market is going through a period of high volatility. Sure, the market started its downward movement even before the tariff tantrums, but the volatility has really spiked since the so-called 'Liberation Day' when all the trade uncertainties began. At one point, the S&P 500 entered into correction territory; however, it was brief, and it recovered quickly. The new Trump administration is reshaping international trade policies with new tariffs on almost every country, in addition to sector-specific tariffs, though most of the reciprocal tariffs (except for China) have been paused for 90 days to allow time for negotiations. Investors and consumers alike are not sure of their near to mid-term impacts, even if some aspects may be good in the long term. It is quite likely that on a 5-10-year horizon, some tariffs may result in better outcomes, helping to re-shore some of the manufacturing and narrow down the trade imbalances, but they are likely to cause hardships and inflationary pressures in the short to mid-term. Also, the ongoing effort to shrink the federal government is likely a good thing, but it still has some short-term impacts on economic growth. The market fears that all this uncertainty may cause consumer spending to go down and businesses to pull back from investments and hiring. Moreover, the markets were already overvalued to begin with, especially in the tech sector, due to all the AI-related hype, and needed to pull back.

To make things worse, there is quite a bit of uncertainty with regard to the direction of interest rates. The Fed is holding rates steady between 4.25% and 4.50%, at least for now. The relatively high interest rates are causing a lot of problems in many sectors of the economy, especially housing. We think the Fed needs to be flexible and ready to compensate for the additional burden that consumers may face from the new tariff regime.

You can see the odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-wk high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any of the duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: AVGO, BRO, CI, CTAS, CTRA, DKS, HEI, and MLI (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: HWM (2 duplicates).

We are now left with 40 (50 - 10) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes MOS, DKS, CTRA, IMO, and BAC (leaving us 35 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes QSR, leaving us with 34 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. This removes LLY, SF, and MSFT, leaving us with 31 names.

Finally, we will remove some stocks to avoid over-concentration from one sector or industry segment. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep BCS and BBVA but remove CBSH and HSBC.

Computer, Electronics, and Business Software: We will keep SSNC but remove MSCI and APH.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO.

Finance – Insurance: We keep BRO but remove ERIE.

Finance – Inv. Banking: We will keep GS and GL but remove MS.

Industrial- Const. & Engg.: We keep HWM and CTAS but remove GWW.

Gold: We keep RGLD.

Utility: We keep NRG but remove VST.

REITs: We keep SBAC but remove AMH.

We also remove some of the stocks with the lowest relative strength in the list. We also remove PWR and TXRH.

We are now left with 20 names.

In all, we removed eleven names, so we are finally left with 20 (31-11) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(HEI), (FIX), (BBVA), (BCS), (FOX), (SSNC), (KNSL), (DFS), (GS), (GL), (MA), (HWM), (CI), (BRO), (RGLD), (MLI), (SBAC), (AVGO), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Table 2: Top 20 High Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then on the decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is not a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (April 2025):

(SSNC), (DFS), (GL), (MA), (HWM), (BRO), (RGLD), (MLI), (CTAS), and (NRG).

Previous Month Selections:

(BK), (SSNC), (DFS), (V), (HWM), (BRO), (RGLD), (MLI), (AVGO), and (NRG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly.

Note: Please note that if a stock was in a previous month's list but is no longer selected in the current list, it does not mean the stock is no longer a good choice. If the stock still scores an HG-Quality score of >70, we think it could still be at least a "hold" (if not a "buy"). However, for new selections, we take into account many more factors than just the HG-Quality score. The purpose here is to highlight the top candidates every month. Please conduct further research and due diligence before making buy or sell decisions.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Past Performance

Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Fund (VIG). Some of our selections did not have a history going back to 2008, so we are only providing the comparison from 2011. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 14 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023:

Since the inception of this series, we have provided the progress and performance of the series. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG), and also with the S&P500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of Jan. 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio. As expected, the performance this month is muted but not negative, even though both VIG and S&P500 had significant negative returns.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks

Overall, we had a decent positive performance from last month's portfolio and ended with positive returns of 1.15%, while the benchmarks VIG and SPY both had significant negative returns. We had a significant positive movement and profitability from Royal Gold (RGLD) and NRG Energy (NRG). However, three stocks, Bank of New York Mellon (BK), SS&C Technology (SSNC), and Mueller Industries (MLI), topped in the negative territory. This month, we are retaining a total of seven stocks from last month but replacing the other three. The new entrants are Globe Life (GL) and Cintas (CTAS), which are replacing BK, and the semiconductor giant Broadcom (AVGO). Also, Mastercard (MA) is replacing Visa (V), even though they are quite similar and equally attractive.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not buy recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially at the present time. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: Itron: The AI‑Driven Backbone Of The Smart Metering
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In most of my previous articles on the information technology sector (XLK), I look at companies that have already begun integrating AI into their products or services, and the effect that such integration has had on their business models. And so far, most of the companies that I have analyzed, have had a positive impact on their business operations, profitability, and growth. This is no different from Itron (NASDAQ:ITRI), a relatively small company worth $4.63B in market cap. The company specializes as a smart-meter vendor and has proven to be a global leader in energy, water, electricity, smart cities, IIoT, and intelligent infrastructure services.

What's interesting about ITRI is how it has been positioning itself as an industrial IoT leader since it conducted a restructuring in 2016. And for sure, it seems the restructure has paid off. The restructure positioned ITRI as one of the most established companies in the metering market. Previously, it only supplied electric meters and software to utility firms across the United States. However, it has grown to have over 8,000 customers in more than 100 countries. Their next leg of growth is the integration of AI into their product portfolio. This is an expansion from their AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure), introducing an extra layer of Edge AI Leadership and data flywheel. This addition has enabled savings on power, time-of-day billing, and power regulation among other efficient improvements that help in providing, regulating, and billing electricity usage.

These advancements have had a positive impact on the company's revenues. First and foremost, the company has been able to increase its revenues by 13% and expects to increase by 25% by 2027. Secondly, AI now commands between 70% and 80% of their gross margins. As many utilities are now on the path to digital transformation, products that have embedded AI have become critical and also add an annuity-style revenue segment. These are some of the reasons that make me believe that the company is poised for great growth prospects.

Company Overview

Itron is a leader in grid edge intelligence, energy, water management, and smart city applications. The company is based in Washington, US and has over 5,000 employees across the globe. The company's business model is innovating new ways for utilities and cities to manage energy and water. 68% of their revenue is derived from network solutions while 82% of their sales are from North America as shown in Figure 1 below.

The Catalyst: An Expanding Addressable Market

In the current era of advanced tech, public utilities and municipalities around the world are facing rapid changes around demand, affordability, sustainability, and reliability. According to the European Environment Agency, cities are increasingly using natural resources and energy to sustain the daily lives and activities of the urban population. This means that managing resources such as energy and water is becoming a big topic of discussion. The United Nations has projected that 68% of the global population will live in urban areas by 2050. In its report, it stresses the importance of sustainable utilization and use of resources.

The push for sustainable and efficient use of resources is ITRI's top priority at the moment. With the rise of data centers and the growth of AI, there is a corresponding strain on aging infrastructure. According to a report by Vox, AI is already using as much energy as a small country and expects the energy needed to support data storage facilities to double by 2026. Additionally, the International Energy Authority (IIE), projects that data centers will increase global energy consumption from 160 TWh in 2022 up to 590 TWh by 2026, as shown in figure 2.

With such high energy demands, certainly, the consumption of electricity, water, and other utilities will definitely increase. As a result, this will have a positive effect on the smart metering market and the Industrial IoT (IIoT) market. A report by Mondor Intelligence shows that the smart meter market is expected to grow at an average CAGR of 8.14% to reach $257.78B as shown in Figure 3.

On the other side, the Industrial IoT market is expected to reach $286.3B growing at an average of 8.1% CAGR. This 8.1% will be a result of internet penetration globally, the adoption of cloud platforms, and increased adoption of IPv6 as shown in Figure 4.

If you combine both markets, the combined total addressable market (TAM) is expected to reach $543B by 2029 as shown below in Figure 5.

Market 2024 Size 2029 Forecast CAGR
Smart Meter Market $174.31B $257.78B 8.14%
Industrial IoT Market (IIoT) $194.4B $286.3B 8.1%
Total Combined TAM $368.7B $544.1B

With a combined TAM of roughly $544B in the next couple of years, Itron's 2024 revenues of $2.4B represent approximately a 0.4% penetration rate. This shows you that ITRI is well-positioned to capitalize on these industry trends and still has a lot of potential to gain market share.

Valuation

From SA Quant ratings, as shown in Figure 6, we can see that the company is performing way better than the sector median. With a Revisions Grade, the company has 9 upgrades in the last 90 days as compared to zero downgrades. This tells you that this company is on the radar of many institutional buyers hence creating attention.

In the last 2 years, the company has beaten analysts' EPS estimates 8 times, while revenue beats have been 5 times.

From a valuation point of view, the company is trading at a forward PEG ratio of 1.18x while that of the sector median is at 1.36x, representing a 13.43% discount. On the other side, the forward price/sales of Itron is at 1.88x representing a 19.82% discount as shown in Figure 8 below.

With regard to growth, the company's growth prospects are in line with the industry's projected growth. This implies that with the presence of revenue growth, the ability to drive margins higher through R&D provides a certain degree of optimism that the company is undervalued, and the market has not factored in the growth prospects of the company.

With regard to Peer comparison, Itron is trading relatively lower than its peers from an EV/EBITDA perspective. While ITRI is trading at 15.20x, Vontier Corp. (VNT) is trading at 9.32x, Novanta (NOVT) is trading at 24.48x, Cognex Corp. (CGNX) is trading at 25.81x, Badger Meter (BMI) and VusionGroup (OTCPK:SRBEF) are trading at 26.93x and 49.3x, respectively, as shown in Figure 10 below in Figure 9.

Itron's Key Risk

One of the biggest risks, which has also been with the company, is currency risk. With the aggressive expansion plans into other countries and regions, the near-term numbers could be affected as a result of this. However, as we have seen, the market is expanding, and it is huge. Enough for all players.

Final Thoughts

It's quite clear that the TAM in smart metering and IIoT is expanding. Itron has been a market leader for so many years now. As we have seen, utilities, data centers, AI, and urbanization will be on the rise in the next couple of years. As a market leader, ITRI should be able to grow by simply maintaining its market share and position.

data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street on Thursday posted a loss for the holiday-shortened week, with traders experiencing more of the volatile, topsy-turvy trading that has become the norm recently.

Stock markets were shut on Good Friday.

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) has now notched seven negative weeks out of nine, as tariff developments continue to sour sentiment. After several - sometimes confusing - adjustments and clarifications to tariff policy last week, things were quieter on that front over the past few days. Instead, U.S. President Donald Trump and other officials made public remarks about positive trade negotiations with multiple countries except China.

The U.S. and China's trade war continued to heat up after chipmakers Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD) disclosed anticipated charges due to U.S. export controls on some of their products to the Asian nation. The updates, along with a weak quarterly bookings performance by Dutch chip giant ASML (ASML), led to a tech rout and contributed to the majority of the week's overall market losses.

A hawkish Federal Reserve chair also played his part. Jerome Powell at the Economic Club of Chicago, expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on the central bank's dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment. Trump later criticized him for not lowering interest rates, and has been said to be mulling over whether to remove Powell.
