data.title: Netflix: A Recession Will Not Take Down This King (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Netflix, (NASDAQ:NFLX), published in January 2025, I examined the company’s Q4 earnings report and investigated whether the surge in memberships seen in Q4 was a one-off or was a sign of things to come. I also analysed whether the company had made any meaningful progress on its ads business. I had a HOLD rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has gained 1.59%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500, which declined 13.06% during the same period.

In this article, I examine the company’s first quarter report, the first one without any membership metrics, and analyse how the company has been coping with the macroeconomic uncertainties and whether the company’s growth was impeded on account of the macro headwinds.

First Quarter Highlights

NFLX had a very strong start to the year, with both the top- and bottom-line estimates handily beating analyst estimates. Q1 revenues of $10.54 billion were up 12.51% y/y and beat analyst estimates by $39.44 million. Diluted EPS came in at $6.61, up 25.2% y/y, beating analyst estimates by $0.93. Operating margins came in very strong once again, expanding by 360 bps (3.6%), at 31.7%. The company also generated free cash flows of $2.6 billion, a substantial jump from the $2.1 billion generated in the first quarter of last year.

The guidance for the second quarter was also very strong in my opinion. More specifically, Q2 revenues are expected to grow 15.4% y/y, and operating margins are expected to come in at 33.3%, which would be a substantial jump from the 27.2% that the company generated in the second quarter of last year. Finally, there were no tariff-related pressures facing the company, as NFLX reaffirmed its FY25 guidance, which projects revenues to come in between $43.5 billion and $44.5 billion, and operating margins to come in at 29%.

NFLX Demonstrates Its Ability to Withstand A Recession

If there is one key takeaway for investors from NFLX’s Q1 report, it is that the company has all the ingredients to shield itself from any form of recession or economic slowdown. And I am not basing this on Q1 report alone, but for what the company has planned for in the quarters to come. Firstly, the company’s content slate is getting stronger with each passing year. Q1 was no different, as demonstrated by the widespread viewership for several of its titles. Examples include Adolescence (124 million views), Back in Action starring Cameron Diaz and Jamie Foxx, which at 146 million views became the company’s sixth most popular English language film ever, and international releases such as Ad Vitam from France (63 million views), and Counterattack from Mexico (59 million views), both of which were placed in the top-10 of the company’s all-time popular non-English language films. In addition to this, the company also tested new formats such as the toddler learning series Ms Rachel, which at 29 million views, was consistently in the global Top 10. Finally, there’s the company’s partnership with WWE, which has been a stellar success, as Monday Night RAW ranked in the Weekly Top 10 list in 29 different nations and has also maintained a spot in the Global Weekly Top 10 list every week since its debut.

Looking forward, there is absolutely no evidence that indicates that this momentum is about to run out of steam any time soon, recession or not. For instance, in India, from April 1, NFLX became the exclusive home for WWE, with the option of Hindi commentary. This is a significant step in my opinion, one that I would expect would contribute to the company’s growth in its APAC market, a region that has been seeing strong revenue growth with each passing quarter (revenue growth, on a constant currency basis, has jumped from 14% y/y in Q1 of last year to 26% y/y in Q1 of this year). Furthermore, Q2 would have more content that has the potential to be popular among viewers across the world. These include new movies such as Nonnas starring Vince Vaughn, a new series such as Straw, starring Taraji P. Henson and The Four Seasons, starring Steve Carell and Tina Fey. Add to this the likes of the series finale of You and Big Mouth, and new seasons of Black Mirror and The Dallas Cowboys Cheerleaders. Finally, in Q3, the company plans to stream the Taylor vs Serrano boxing rematch, after the first match became the “most-watched professional women’s sports event in US history.” Q3 will also see the series finale of Squid Games. And at some point during this year, there will be the series finale of Stranger Things, which the management didn’t even mention in their Q1 letter to shareholders.

This steady stream of high-quality content should shield NFLX from any macroeconomic headwinds such as a recession. Moreover, even if there is a recession in the U.S., NFLX would be entering such an environment for the first time with an ad-supported plan, which means that consumers would have a choice of opting for a lower-priced plans as they weather the recessionary storm. This would ensure that churn is minimized.

Overall, I don’t see a scenario where NFLX loses out due to any form of macroeconomic headwinds, including one where nations impose levies on services. The company’s content slate has been a stellar success, has attracted subscribers and, has also ensured a higher retention rate, even as these consumers were cutting down on other discretionary items. Given that the quality of its content slate isn’t deteriorating in the coming quarters, there is no reason to believe that consumer would start cancelling their subscriptions even in the event of a recession.

Two days prior to the company’s earnings release, there was a Wall Street Journal report that mentioned that NFLX aims to become a $1 trillion company by 2030 and also aims to double its revenues to approximately $78 billion by 2030, including $9 billion in global ad sales by 2030. When investors look at the company’s history of producing high-quality content, including iconic animation franchises, and when you factor in the company’s growing gaming business as well as a steadily progressing ads business, these “ambitious” targets are very much achievable within the next five years. A recession, global or US-focused, is not going to stop that.

Valuation

Forward P/E Multiple Approach

Price Target

$1095

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

36.2x (see Image Below)

Projected FY26 EPS

$30.25

Source: LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), Author’s Projections, and Company’s Q1FY25 Letter to Shareholders

As mentioned earlier, the company re-affirmed its FY25 guidance, which means revenues are expected to come in between $43.5 and $44.5 billion. The company has already exceeded its own Q1 revenue guidance, which increases the odds that the company would at the very minimum, hit the high-end of the full-year guidance. As such, I am not making any changes to my revenue projections from last time, that is, I continue to assume that FY25 revenues would come in at $44.5 billion.

Based on management’s guidance, operating margins for FY25 are expected to come in at 29%. The company’s operating margins for Q1 exceeded its own expectations, coming in at 31.7%. Q2 operating margins are now expected to come in at 33.3%. I was tempted to raise my operating margin guidance, but management did highlight that the content-related expenses are projected to significantly “grow and ramp” in Q3 and Q4. The increase in spending makes sense given that some big-ticket items are launching in these quarters, which include the likes of Taylor vs Serrano boxing rematch, the Christmas Day NFL games, as well as a heavier film slate in Q4. All things considered, I am maintaining my operating margin projection at 29%, which means the projected operating income for FY25 remains at $12.9 billion.

I have assumed FY25 non-operating expenses for the company to be $662 million, which would be approximately the average net non-operating expenses in the last five years, according to LSEG Data. The non-operating expenses for FY24 were $452 million. If one looks at Q1 non-operating expenses, they were $18 million. Q1 non-operating expenses for FY25 already amounted to $133 million, which implies that overall non-operating expenses would be significantly higher than $452 million. So $662 million makes sense in my opinion. I have maintained the same projected tax rate at 12.6%. Taken together, this would result in a total FY25 net income of $10.7 billion.

According to the company’s latest income statement (unaudited), on account of repurchases, the weighted average shares of common stock used to calculate the diluted EPS has come down to 436.9 million from 437.8 million. Using this figure, FY25 diluted EPS is projected to come in at $24.49, marginally higher than my previous estimate of $24.44. This represents a y/y EPS growth of 23.5%.

Given the cocktail of tailwinds that the company has, despite the macro uncertainties, in the form of a strong content slate (films, TV shows, live sports & programming), its steadily growing advertising business, and more room to capture in the market (NFLX engagement, despite the growth, still represents less than 10% of the TV hours), I am of the opinion that the company should continue to grow its bottom-line at double-digits in FY26 as well. I have therefore assumed FY26 EPS growth to be the same as FY25, which is 23.5%, slightly lower than its mean long-term EPS growth of 25.3%.

According to Seeking Alpha, NFLX’s forward PEG ratio currently stands at 1.59, slightly above its 5-year average of 1.49. The table below presents two scenarios, the first of which assumes a forward PEG ratio of 1.59 and an EPS growth of 25.3%. The second assumes a forward PEG ratio of 1.49 and an EPS growth of 25.3%.

Based on the above table, my new price target for NFLX is $1095, higher than my previous PT of $946, and which suggests an upside of about 12.5% from current levels. In addition to the significant upside from current levels, NFLX stock also has strong momentum, as evidenced by the A+ grade on Seeking Alpha’s Momentum Scale. Even the short-term momentum, despite the volatility, remains at A-. Considering both the upside and the momentum, I am upgrading my rating on the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

Risk Factors

The primary risk factor to my bull case is the scenario where the company’s ads business doesn’t demonstrate the strong growth it is projected to achieve in the coming quarters. The company plans to roll out its own ad tech platform globally soon. However, any hiccups in the rollout could have a negative impact on the segment’s growth, which would subsequently have a negative impact on its overall growth trajectory.

The other risk factor is the threat from YouTube. During the earnings call, management reiterated that their current priority is to focus on capturing the approximately 80% of the TV time that “neither Netflix nor YouTube have today.”

According to data from CNBC, as shown above, YouTube continues to dominate viewership engagement, with its TV streaming growth jumping 23.7% y/y compared to NFLX, which saw a decline of about 2.5%. While the management may have downplayed the head-to-head battle with YouTube, the disparity in growth is something that investors would need to watch out for. Any lack of progress on this front would also be a detriment to the company’s long-term growth.

Concluding Thoughts

It was yet another stellar quarter from NFLX, beating on both the top- and bottom-lines. The company once again demonstrated its ability to manage its expenses, as evidenced by the strong operating margins and free cash flows. The company also released a content slate that generated strong viewership engagement. More positive progress was seen in its ads business, and its partnership with WWE has also shown early signs of success.

With a stronger content slate set to be released in the coming quarters, including series finales of some of its fan-favourites, and with the company planning to roll out its ad tech platform to more markets outside the US soon, NFLX approaches a challenging macroenvironment from a position of strength. Moreover, given that it has lower-priced ad-supported plans, the odds of cancellations are much lower during these turbulent periods, compared to previous downturns.

From a valuation perspective, NFLX is attractive once again, with significant upside from current levels and a strong momentum. As such, I have upgraded the stock from a HOLD to a BUY.

There might be a recession, there might not be a recession. NFLX, to me, has proven that it is an all-weather company. Naturally, the stock has also become one to own for all occasions.

data.title: 3 Never-Sell Dividend Gems I Expect To Easily Beat The Market
data.description:
Introduction

"Very suddenly."

I think that's a good way to describe what happened on the stock market in the first few months of this year. What started as an uptrend until mid-February quickly turned into a market that briefly fell more than 20%.

Triggered by tariffs and fears of a widespread global recession, we see the same reversal in economic soft data, which is based on leading economic indicators.

Goldman Sachs' business optimism tracker, for example, collects data from almost every leading indicator in the United States, as the very long footnote under the chart below shows.

After more than a year of rising optimism, the indicator is now telling us to fasten our seatbelts.

This sentiment is mirrored by the Bank of America Fund Manager Survey, which shows that after President Trump's 'Liberation Day' press conference, nearly half of them predict a hard landing, the highest number in at least two years.

The worst part is that tariff uncertainty comes with a wave of geopolitical and macroeconomic problems, as the United States needs to refinance a lot of short-term debt. As I wrote in a recent in-depth article, it needs longer-term rates to come down for that. This, however, is challenging, as an elevated budget deficit and inflation risks (stemming from, i.e., tariffs) make bonds relatively unattractive.

As a result, uncertainty has driven investors into hard assets like gold, which has substantially outperformed the S&P 500 in recent months.

Looking at the Bloomberg chart below, we see that the S&P 500-to-gold ratio hasn't been this low since the depths of the 2020 pandemic.

Based on this context, it's fair to say that none of this is great news.

However, I take all of this with a grain of salt.

While I am fully aware of the economic risks, which I have described in countless articles, I also believe that leading indicators like surveys are not as dependable as they usually are.

For example, nobody really knows what the next few weeks, days, or even months will look like, as so much depends on White House decisions. Let's say the President were to reach an agreement with China (whatever that could look like), tariffs could come down and allow CEOs to plan with more confidence.

Or to put it differently, I think this is the kind of environment where the market is just one tweet from the President away from a 5% rally (or slump).

It also needs to be said that whenever the market is in a state of fear, a buying opportunity is often not far away. Currently, global fund manager sentiment is close to the COVID lows, which, I think, is ridiculous.

These are the same people who are now expecting to go massively underweight in U.S. equities, as the chart below shows. However, note that this shows intent to cut equity exposure in the United States. It needs to be seen just how many of them will really sell, especially after the U.S. has already underperformed global equities this year.

While I have often made the case that the U.S. market, which is overweight FANG+ stocks, is prone to some portfolio adjustments, I believe the sentiment shift has gone way too far.

But then again, a further escalating trade war could easily cause a recession.

Although it may look like investing is like flipping a coin, I stick to what works best, which is buying high-quality companies in a bearish environment.

Historically speaking, that's the best way to win.

Now, let me show you a few stocks that I own and have neglected for way too long: dividend growers that I expect to outperform the market for many years to come.

Deere & Company (DE) - A Cyclical Player With A Special Something

Let's start with the stock pick that gives me the highest chance of running into issues with some readers.

So, let's get these things out of the way first.

  • Deere & Company yields just 1.4%.
  • The company is not 'obviously' cheap.

The good news is that this somewhat low yield packs a punch, supported by its business model, as Deere has spent the past decades building one of the most impressive footprints in global agriculture, selling premium brands in areas like production and precision agriculture (large tractors/combines), small agriculture and turf, and construction.

While its brand may be underrepresented in nations like India, where smaller tractors are more popular, it is a leader in North America, South America, and Europe, where it grows in a $150 billion addressable market through investments in automation, AI, and other tools that improve efficiencies for farmers.

Deere has been a leader in next-gen farming for decades, which is one of the reasons why it has strong pricing power.

Moreover, the company, which has the habit of keeping dividends unchanged during times of turmoil, has a fantastic dividend track record. While it may yield just 1.4%, the dividend has more than doubled since 2020.

On December 3, 2024, the dividend was hiked by 10.2%. It still enjoys a payout ratio of 27% and comes with a side of aggressive buybacks, as Deere has bought back close to a fifth of its shares over the past ten years.

The recent dividend hike is good news, as the company has been under pressure from industry headwinds. During its latest 1Q25 earnings call, the company noted that in the U.S. and Canada, large ag equipment sales are expected to decline by roughly 30% this year, which reflects elevated interest rates, macroeconomic uncertainty, and high levels of used inventory.

That was before Trump's tariff plans surfaced.

'Luckily' for the company, just 5% of its complete goods sales come from Mexico. Only 2% of its manufacturing costs in the United States rely on goods sourced from China.

With regard to its outlook, I have had a $600 price tag on Deere for many years. It returned 14.8% per year since October 2004.

When Deere traded close to $300 in 2022, my target looked like a wild target. Now, Deere trades only 32% below that target at a blended P/E ratio of 20.0x

In November, analysts saw a path to $31 in 2027 EPS. Now, that number is slightly above $25. If we avoid a recession, I expect a path to $31, supported by sticky inflation (pricing power), tight global agriculture inventories, and the equipment replacement cycle, which was interrupted by the aforementioned challenges.

Applying a 20x multiple would pave the road for a target of at least $600 per share.

As we can see below, Deere has consistently beaten the S&P 500, with temporary underperformance during times of economic headwinds.

The next one is a bit less controversial.

Extra Space Storage (EXR) - A Hard-To-Beat REIT

I believe it's essential to buy companies with moats, which protect them against competition and tend to come with benefits like pricing power.

Unfortunately, REITs don't usually have moats, as the only thing that tends to keep competitors at bay is access to capital. With enough capital, almost everything is possible in real estate.

To make matters worse, self-storage companies tend to have even more competition, as it is very easy to build storage. It's usually just a big building with many smaller boxes.

Nonetheless, EXR is one of my favorite investments and a REIT that has done very well, as it has the highest 20-year return among self-storage REITs and the third-best total return among all public REITs.

Nowadays, the company operates more than 4,000 properties covering 308 million square feet of rentable space. It has a 14.7% market share, which makes it the leader in a market that is still highly fragmented due to private and institutional non-REIT players, as we can see below.

It also helps that, despite having no moat, the self-storage industry has plenty of opportunities, which is what attracted me to these assets in the first place:

  • Steady demand: As people continue to buy stuff they don't need and face housing changes, self-storage facilities often see consistent demand, regardless of economic fluctuations, although a weakening housing market tends to cause short-term headwinds.

  • Low maintenance costs: Compared to other types of real estate investments, self-storage typically requires less maintenance, as tenants do not live in their self-storage boxes. It's far from the nightmares that some residential owners have to deal with.
  • High profit margins: Self-storage businesses often have attractive profit margins, with relatively low operational costs compared to revenue potential. After all, once a building has been built, it's easy to take care of all the tenants.
  • Other uses: I believe self-storage will play an increasing role as micro storage for businesses, especially in light of e-commerce and last-mile logistics, as self-storage assets are often very close to residential hotspots.
  • Pricing: Although REITs, in general, have pricing power through mark-to-market price adjustments, self-storage assets have quick price adjustments, as tenants usually do not stay very long.

That's why self-storage growth rates have been elevated fairly consistently, as we can see below. The data also shows that EXR beats its peers almost every single year when it comes to same-store revenue growth.

With regard to its dividend, EXR yields 4.7%.

This dividend has been unchanged since 2023, which makes sense, as that's when the housing market started to weaken. Just like Deere, Extra Space Storage keeps its dividend consistent during challenging times, followed by aggressive growth when earnings improve.

Over the past ten years, the dividend has more than doubled, which sets it apart from many other REITs as well.

Although it will likely require an upswing in consumer sentiment and home sales to kickstart EXR (and its peers) again, I like the risk/reward and have consistently added to my position.

Valuation-wise, EXR trades at 18.3x adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO"), which is below its multi-decade average of 20.3x due to sluggish growth.

After contraction in 2023 and 2024, analysts see a path to higher growth, expecting 1%, 5%, and 4% per-share AFFO growth in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively.

Conservatively speaking, this paves the road for 11-12% annual returns.

Caterpillar (CAT) - Recession Or Not, CAT Has Gotten Attractive

I don't own any mining stocks.

Instead, I bought Caterpillar as a 'picks and shovels' play, as the company usually has more pricing power than its customers and less operational risk.

The chart below compares the Caterpillar stock price to the average price of gold and copper (green line), which are two of the most important commodities for the mining industry.

We see two things:

  • Generally speaking, higher commodity prices bode well for Caterpillar, as the two lines almost move in lockstep.
  • Currently, there's a steep divergence, as Caterpillar has been sold due to tariff and (related) recession fears.

That's an opportunity, as the current 30% sell-off means two things. First, if the economy avoids a recession, Caterpillar could be incredibly cheap. Second, even if a recession were to occur, a lot would have been priced in.

In other words, the risk/reward has gotten good, either way.

While total demand uncertainty is elevated, the company continues to improve its business, as its service segment is growing rapidly. Last year, for example, service revenues grew to $24 billion. That's a record and 4% higher on a year-over-year basis.

Even more important, total service sales are now 37% of total sales.

To support future growth, the company is leveraging digital tools, including its VisionLink telematics platform and generative AI for service recommendations, to improve customer uptime and loyalty.

More than two-thirds of new equipment sales included Customer Value Agreements ("CVAs"), which lock in recurring revenue streams. E-commerce platforms also saw record adoption, with streamlined onboarding for digital products.

Note that the company has an installed base of 1.5 million connected assets, which is great for service/maintenance revenue and a valuable source of data.

With that said, last year, despite lower sales (-3%), adjusted EPS made a new high (+3%), as CAT has a fantastic track record of shareholder returns. This includes buybacks.

Over the past ten years, CAT shares have returned 341%, beating the S&P 500 by almost 140 points. During this period, it has bought back slightly more than a fifth of its shares.

It also has a fantastic dividend track record.

Currently yielding 2.0%, CAT has a payout ratio of a mere 25% and a track record of more than 30 consecutive years. Its five-year CAGR is 7.0%.

Last year alone, more than $10 billion was returned to shareholders, slightly more than 100% of its $9.4 billion free cash flow result. Nearly $8 billion of these distributions came in the form of buybacks.

With regard to its valuation, Caterpillar shares trade at a mere 13.6x earnings, three points below its long-term average.

This year, EPS is expected to decline by 10%, potentially followed by 10-11% annual growth through at least 2027.

While these numbers are all subject to change, I like the risk/reward a lot and believe CAT has exactly what it takes to beat the market going forward, especially if the rotation from growth to value stocks continues.

Depending on my financial situation, I will add to my CAT investment a bit more aggressively, as it has become my smallest position.

Takeaway

The market's sudden downturn this year, fueled by tariffs, recession fears, and geopolitical uncertainty, has left investors scrambling for answers and some kind of certainty.

Leading indicators, from Goldman's optimism tracker to BofA's fund manager survey, scream caution, as it seems, we're at the start of one of the worst recessions in decades.

However, I'm skeptical of that.

While I have spent countless hours writing articles on economic challenges and my view on the future, it needs to be said that sentiment swings this extreme often precede opportunities, not disasters.

As a result, I'm sticking to high-quality names that I expect to continue what they do best, which is beating the market. On top of that, all dividend stocks in this article have a focus on rewarding their shareholders, which is also something I expect to last.

So, what's the bottom line?

Despite uncertainty, I think it makes sense to focus on high-quality stocks that can help us reach our financial goals.

data.title: Unity Bancorp: Quality At A Decent Price
data.description:
As somebody who is curious by nature, I am always looking for new and interesting opportunities to dig into. This ultimately led me to come across a bank by the name of Unity Bancorp (UNTY). With a market capitalization as of this writing of $401.7 million, the institution is certainly on the small side of the spectrum. However, I really prefer to analyze banks that are anywhere from $200 million to $5 billion, so this was a natural fit for me. Digging into the enterprise, I can see that shares are a bit more expensive than I would like them to be on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible book basis. However, the consistent growth the enterprise has achieved in recent years, combined with its high asset quality and low price-to-earnings multiple, makes it a compelling opportunity. At the end of the day, I do believe that this is a good ‘buy’ candidate for those that are interested in banking prospects.

A small bank to consider

If you're not familiar with Unity Bancorp, the company operates as a small bank holding firm incorporated out of New Jersey. From its headquarters, the business operates 21 bank branches that are mostly located along the Route 22/Route 78 corridors in parts of New Jersey and in Northampton County, Pennsylvania. The business also has access, including through its banking locations and its online platform, to clients in the New York City metropolitan area.

Over the years, the company has operated like many other small banks have. Most of its revenue currently comes from net interest income that it generates from the spread between the interest it gets on its loans and securities, and how much it has to pay out to depositors and lenders. Naturally, this means that the company takes on deposits. And using those funds, it originates commercial, SBA, consumer, mortgage, home equity, and personal loans. It also lends for the commercial and residential construction industries. On the non-interest side of things, the enterprise also generates revenue from stop-payment fees, wire transfer fees, debit card income, interchange fees, foreign ATM fees, and more.

Over the last few years, management has done a fine job of growing the firm from a balance sheet perspective. From 2022 to 2024, deposits expanded from $1.79 billion to $2.10 billion. Whenever I look at banks, especially small banks, I like to see how much of their deposit growth has come from brokered deposits. These are deposits that are lent to the company by other financial firms. There is no guarantee that they will be there for the business tomorrow and they carry higher interest rates than other forms of deposits. Small banks use them in order to grow, but they are a costly way to grow compared to organic deposits. Over the same three years that I looked at already, brokered deposits grew from $154.6 million to $217.9 million. If we strip this out from the equation, we would get total deposit growth from $1.63 billion to $1.88 billion. That is still an impressive showing as I see it.

This expansion on the deposit front has empowered the company to grow other parts of its balance sheet also. From 2022 through 2024, loans on the company's books expanded from $2.08 billion to $2.23 billion. The largest chunk of its loan portfolio includes the $1.09 billion dedicated to commercial real estate. This excludes $130.2 million of commercial real estate construction loans. The company also boasts $630.9 million in residential mortgage loans, as well as small amounts of SBA loans, consumer loans, other types of commercial loans, and residential construction loans.

Other aspects of the balance sheet also deserve attention. From 2022 through 2024, the company has seen the value of securities on its books remain in a fairly narrow range of between $135.7 million and $145 million. Cash and cash equivalents managed to rise from $114.8 million to $180.4 million. Over the same timeframe that we are talking about, it is noteworthy that debt on the company's books actually contracted from $393.3 million to $230.8 million. This is always nice to see and should prove accretive to the company's bottom line.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen consistent growth in recent years. Over the last three years to be precise, net interest income expanded from $85.8 million to $94.7 million. This is in spite of the fact that the firm's net interest margin fell from 4.40% to 4.16%. This kind of contraction is not shocking to me. At a time when interest rates are elevated, banks have to compete with not only each other, but also alternative investment opportunities, for deposits. This usually results in a decline in net interest margin.

Historically, only a small portion of the company's revenue has come from non-interest activities. Examples here include interchange fees, debit card income, and more. During the window of time that is relevant to this analysis, Unity Bancorp saw its non-interest income expand slightly from $8 million to $8.5 million. This growth, combined with the rise in net interest income, allowed overall net profits to expand from $38.5 million to $41.5 million. This is not rapid growth by any means, and it can be attributed to higher operating expenses. But in this kind of environment that we have seen in recent years, I would take any growth over no growth.

When it comes to valuation, shares are on the cheaper end of the spectrum relative to earnings. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced on a price-to-earnings basis. The 9.7 reading that the business boasts is near the high end of the 6 to 10 range that I like to see in this industry. However, as the chart also shows, only one of the five companies that I compared the business to is cheaper than it is on this basis. The opposite is true when we are talking about both the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see how the enterprise stacks up against these similar firms. In fact, it ended up being the most expensive of the group.

Normally, this would be a turn-off as far as I am concerned. But I do make exceptions when asset quality is high. In the first chart below, you can see the company's return on assets. The reading of 1.68% that the business has is high on an absolute basis and is higher than any of these other firms that I compared it to. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same thing regarding return on equity. The 14.99% that the company has is comfortably higher than any of the other five comparable businesses. This demonstrates asset quality that is worthy of attention.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I am a fan of Unity Bancorp. Shares are attractively priced relative to earnings, though they are a bit pricey when it comes to book value and tangible book value. Asset quality is elevated, and the business has seen consistent growth from a revenue perspective, a profit perspective, and a balance sheet perspective. Due to these factors, I have no problem rating the company a ‘buy’ at this time.

data.title: SentinelOne: One Of The Better Growth Names To Buy Today
data.description:
SentinelOne (NYSE:S) has not been immune to the tariff-driven selloff. Wall Street may have been disappointed by management’s conservative guidance for this upcoming fiscal year. It appears that the low valuation has offered some downside support, with the net cash balance sheet also helping to sweeten the deal. SentinelOne has thus far not been able to capitalize on CrowdStrike’s (CRWD) outages but at these valuations, I see the stock performing quite well even so. I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy rating.

SentinelOne Stock Price

I last covered SentinelOne in September where I rated the stock a buy on account of the potential long-term benefits of the Lenovo deal. The stock has fallen around 33% since then.

The more attractive valuation has led to a ratings upgrade as growth investors can seek to take advantage of the ongoing market volatility.

SentinelOne Stock Key Metrics

SentinelOne is a cybersecurity stock focused on endpoint protection. CRWD is the giant in the space, though SentinelOne has tried to frame itself as being more AI-powered with more automated remediation.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 29% YoY revenue growth to $226 million, exceeding guidance for $222 million.

The company saw its non-GAAP operating margin finally turn positive, albeit ever so slightly, at 1%.

I note that while the company has made meaningful progress on improving non-GAAP profitability, it has not made so much progress on a GAAP perspective. Even so, the progress on non-GAAP margins is still valuable as it means that the company is not burning cash.

SentinelOne ended the quarter with $1.1 billion of cash and investments versus no debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the company to see 22% YoY revenue growth in the first quarter and 23% YoY growth for the full-year. I suspect that many investors were disappointed by this guide, as there may have been some hope for a more moderate deceleration in growth rates or even an acceleration given the CRWD incident.

On the conference call, management did note that their “competitive win rates were strong and improved compared to prior quarters,” but it is disappointing that this has not shown in the numbers. Management did note that their guidance was partly influenced by their decision to retire their legacy deception solution with about “$10 million of expected churn.” Excluding this impact, the full-year growth outlook might improve to around 24%. Management also appeared to repeatedly emphasize the conservatism of the guidance, calling it a “good achievable starting point.”

The stock had previously rallied on news of a partnership with Lenovo. Management noted that this partnership is still in the works, with the projected impact expected to be “more back end loaded” and likely “in the next fiscal year.” Investors often like to have something to look forward to in the future, and it is nice to have this Lenovo catalyst on hand for the medium term.

Is SentinelOne Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, SentinelOne traded at just around 5.6x sales.

That multiple is a steep discount to its closest competitor in CRWD, which trades at around 20x sales and other cybersecurity names like Palo Alto Networks (PANW) and Zscaler (ZS), which trade at around 12x sales. In my view, the main difference appears to be both lower profit margins as well as the more narrow product offerings. The company has already achieved positive cash flow generation and I expect an inflection in GAAP profitability over the next 3 years. Executing against margin expansion looks like a clear catalyst for a material re-rating. My valuation target is 9x sales, based on 30% long term net margins and a 30x earnings multiple. From current levels, the stock looks poised to deliver considerable upside in a bullish outcome, with strong returns coming from both ongoing top-line growth (around 20% annually) and multiple expansion (around 50% upside to 9x sales). Sure, GAAP operating losses may offset this, but I estimate the impact as being just around 4% to 5% annually.

SentinelOne Stock Risks

It is possible that SentinelOne is unable to effectively compete against CRWD and others. If this were to occur, not only would the growth story fall apart, but the company may also fail to achieve satisfactory profit margins. SentinelOne might need to widen its product portfolio simply to stay relevant - this process might involve diluting shareholders through expensive M&A. There may be some political risk as the company was recently targeted by the Trump administration, eventually leading to the resignation of senior officer Chuck Krebs. The stock is not “dirt cheap” here and may experience considerable volatility in a market downturn.

SentinelOne Stock Conclusion

The recent market volatility has created attractive buying opportunities for brave investors. SentinelOne looks like a solid pick, as the valuation allows for strong returns as the company executes against boosting profit margins. SentinelOne embodies what I look for among high-quality growth names for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio, and that explains why it is a large core holding. I am upgrading the stock to a strong buy rating.

data.title: Big Mac Vs. CPI: An Alternative View Of U.S. Inflation
data.description:
Inflation and the Price of a Big Mac

As you probably know, the Big Mac index is usually used to compare purchasing power in different countries. However, I think the Big Mac index can also be applied to compare the purchasing power of Americans in different years and use it to estimate official inflation data. So, in this article, I decided to compare Big Mac prices in the US and compare them to the US inflation rate. What I am using is not really a Big Mac index in the usual sense, but will be focused exclusively on the US.

Below you can see a table where I calculated the increase in the price of a Big Mac since 2000 and compared it to the U.S. Consumer Price Index. And you know, the Big Mac has been increasing in price almost all the time at twice the rate of inflation. From 2000 to 2025, the Big Mac has increased in price by 163%, while inflation has cumulatively increased by only 85%.

Such a difference rightly raises the question of how accurate the inflation data are. After all, it is no secret that many people, despite the impressive growth of the economy over 25 years, do not feel richer. And in this contest, I recalculated US GDP, both nominal and per capita, in Big Macs. And what you can see in the table below.

If we count GDP in Big Macs instead of the U.S. dollar, the average American is poorer in 2024 than in 2000. And America's total GDP has grown only slightly over the years, from 4.58 trillion Big Macs in 2000 to 4.88 trillion Big Macs in 2024. Moreover, from 2000 to the pandemic, the economy calculated in Big Macs declined.

Technological Deflation

And here I will try to explain what happens to inflation as I understand it.

Imagine an iPhone 12 in 2020, its base model cost $799. A year later, a new iPhone 13 came out, its price was the same, $799. That said, the iPhone 12, as an older model, likely dropped in price.

In this case, I think inflation calculated that iPhone prices fell because the iPhone 12 got cheaper. The bottom line is that this approach puts downward pressure on inflation.

This effect is called hedonic adjustment.

"Hedonics is the science of trying to work out how much product quality has changed and adjusting inflation to take account of the fact that more expensive products are not just inflation, but also improved quality. It is used in the US and UK calculation of inflation."

And it turns out that at the expense of hedonic adjustment, almost all technological development turns against inflation by underestimating its indicators. And in this connection, we can refer to such an indicator as CPI for Computers, Peripherals, and Smart Home Assistants. This indicator is almost always declining!!!

In the table below, you can see that this index has declined since 2007 from a level of 100 points to 34 points in 2024. And I think this effect is just directly related to the example I described with the iPhone.

This effect, I believe, doesn't just apply to smartphones, but to many other products, all the way down to cars. New products get better over time, but are often sold at the same price as past models. And statistics turn this technological development against inflation in favor of deflation.

So Big Mac, as odd as it may seem, is largely right. And the feelings of people who don't think they've gotten richer over the last 25 years are largely correct.

The Evolution of Inflation Calculation Methodologies and Their Implications

I'm not the only person who has noticed this. There are many who think the same way, and even consider alternative inflation, which is counted using old methodologies when hedonic adjustments were not yet included in the inflation calculation.

Before the 1990s and early 2000s, the methodology for calculating inflation was different. It puts more emphasis on calculating the value of a fixed consumer basket of goods. But modern methodologies have changed.

Inflation now takes into account many additional factors.

Substitution Bias: accounts for consumers switching to cheaper counterparts when prices rise.

Hedonic Adjustment: adjusts prices for improvements in the quality of goods.

Changing Basket of Goods and Services: regularly updates the basket of goods to reflect changing consumer habits.

Owners' Equivalent Rent - OER: uses estimated rents instead of asset prices when measuring housing costs.

And the Big Mac index, which is rising in value at twice the rate of official inflation, clearly shows that current methodologies are flawed, and their imperfections lead to systematic underestimation of inflation.

Economic Growth and Welfare

Against this background, if one considers what I have written on inflation to be fair, one can change one's view on the economy and one's expectations a great deal.

I think that GDP growth at the level of 2% in real terms, taking into account the official inflation rate and GDP deflator, from this point of view, is not growth at all, but stagnation. Growth by a smaller amount means recession. And only when the economy grows by 3% or more does it truly grow.

The entire period from the 2008 crisis to the pandemic can be called a stagnation and crisis. But that period was mitigated by the Fed's loose monetary policy. And with this assessment, those investors and economists who like to count economic cycles can make adjustments. Because the picture of economic cycles will be completely different.

In one of my articles, I wrote that credit in the U.S. is now at a low base, which, if the Fed cuts the Fed Funds rate, could cause a strong credit growth and lead to a new credit growth cycle. And from this angle, if you exclude the trade war that Trump started, the US economy has a huge potential for growth, because if the growth of the economy since 2008 has been less than is believed, then the economy is on a different economic cycle, not at its peak!

In the same way, one can feel differently about the Fed's policy. The stated inflation target of 2% is not a low level at all; in real terms, it could be 4%. And if so, that level is much easier to achieve than it may seem. And part of the reason for this may be that the average P/E in the US has risen since the 90s. After all, under current conditions, it turns out that already at 4% real inflation, the Fed's policy will be very soft. The distortion of inflation makes the adjustment in Fed policy towards easing, because with higher actual inflation, for example, at 4%, the Fed will act as it would with inflation equal to 2%, which would be according to official data.

Yields on bonds and on deposits from this perspective will look completely different. After all, if you discount the rate of inflation by, say, 2%. A level that, I assume, is underestimated in official inflation every year. Even now, 10-year U.S. Treasury bonds have a negative real yield adjusted for inflation. Their yield is now 4.3%, while official inflation was 2.4% according to the latest data. And if we add another 2% to that, real inflation could be as high as 4.4% per annum, which is higher than the yield on 10-year Treasuries. And such a statement of the question completely changes the position of the U.S. national debt. After all, if Treasury bonds have negative yields in real terms, then the problem of the U.S. national debt simply does not exist.

From this position, ordinary people's attitudes toward deposits and other fixed-income instruments may be reconsidered. Because often the interest will not cover the real loss of purchasing power.

And even the value of a company's shares can be looked at differently. Below I have compiled a table where I have recalculated the values of the S&P500 index in value to Big Mac.

And in this picture, the dizzying market upswing of the last 25 years is no longer there.

Summary

Of course, my estimate of a 2% annual inflation distortion is hardly correct either. But the point is that methodologies for calculating inflation are imperfect. And because of this, you have to realize that official inflation does not accurately reflect its real level, which can make many issues look different from what they really are. And I think that this factor should always be kept in mind. It may help you to look at all economic issues from a different angle, and help you to find answers to many questions.

Let me say right away that I do not think that this is done on purpose. After all, our life is multifaceted; there are always many processes in the economy, and it is very difficult to take everything into account. Therefore, in the issue of inflation accounting, it is most likely about the imperfection of inflation calculation methods.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
